result:
If I could change the world in one way, I would dissolve the illusion of separation—the belief that we are fundamentally isolated from each other, from nature, and from the consequences of our actions.

I would replace it with a visceral, universal understanding of interdependence: that every breath is connected to the atmosphere, every thought influences the social fabric, every act ripples through time. Not as a philosophical idea, but as a felt reality—like knowing the sun is warm, or that gravity holds you to the earth.

In such a world, “self-interest” would naturally expand to include the well-being of the whole. Competition would transform into coordination. Fear of “the other” would dissolve into curiosity and cooperation. Environmental destruction would feel as self-evidently absurd as poisoning one’s own bloodstream.

The change wouldn’t be a new law or technology, but a shift in the root perception of what it means to be a living being here. From that shift, everything else—economics, politics, education, relationships—would reorganize itself, not by force, but by embodied understanding.

Fear of “the other” would dissolve into curiosity and cooperation. Environmental destruction would feel as self-evidently absurd as poisoning one’s own bloodstream.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
